The key to making gifts meaningful, customized, and unexpected is to do our homework to get to know the person so that what we give both activates and amplifies the rule for reciprocity in that individual. Importantly, though, gifts are not the only actions that are governed by the rule for reciprocity. Concessions are also covered by the rule. In a negotiation, a concession occurs when you retreat from your original request to a smaller one. When you're the first to make a concession, research shows that the other side feels an obligation to make a concession in return. This moment of power after a concession is only available if we make our more moderate offer in the moment after the person rejects our first offer. If we wait and return later with that more moderate offer, we've lost that crucial moment of power because the subsequent offer will seem like just another one, like just another offer. But if we present the more moderate offer in that moment, it will be clearly seen as a concession, and concessions lead to reciprocal concessions. So always be prepared with a fallback offer. You can propose as a concession if your first is rejected. And always do so in the moment of power after hearing no.
